title: The burden of disease vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: The burden of disease is measured in Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs). DALYs measure the total burden of disease – both from years of life lost due to premature death and years lived with a disability. One DALY equals one lost year of healthy life. Here it is age-standardized and expressed per 100,000 individuals.
caption:
The burden of disease vs. GDP per capita, 2023

The burden of disease is measured in Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs). DALYs measure the total burden of disease – both from years of life lost due to premature death and years lived with a disability. One DALY equals one lost year of healthy life. Here it is age-standardized and expressed per 100,000 individuals.

Across countries in 2023, the relationship between economic output and health burden follows a clear downward trajectory on this scatter plot. The horizontal axis measures GDP per capita in international dollars on a logarithmic scale, while the vertical axis tracks the burden of disease in Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs) per 100,000 people. Circles sized by population represent individual nations, color-coded by continent. The data shows a strong negative association where higher income levels coincide with lower disease burdens. At the upper extreme of the vertical axis, the Central African Republic, Niger, and Chad cluster in the 70,000 to 80,000 DALY range, corresponding to GDP per capita figures below $2,000. Other nations in the highest burden band include Burundi, DR Congo, and Mozambique, all situated in the purple African category with low economic output. Moving toward the middle of the distribution, countries like India, Pakistan, and Myanmar occupy the 40,000 to 50,000 DALY band with GDP per capita between $5,000 and $10,000. Large population centers such as China and Indonesia appear lower on the vertical axis, falling between 20,000 and 30,000 DALYs as their economic output rises toward the $20,000 mark. At the far right of the chart, high-income nations including the United States, Germany, Japan, and Singapore cluster in the bottom-right corner, showing GDP per capita exceeding $50,000 and disease burdens dropping below 20,000 DALYs per 100,000. The visual pattern displays a consistent curve where increases in GDP per capita align with reductions in the total burden of disease.
